title: A narrative review of the current state of circulating tumor cells in prostate cancer diagnostic.
abstract: Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in prostate cancer (PCa) have been an area of interest over the past 35 years. The aim of this narrative review is to summarize data on CTC application in PCa diagnosis. Several CTC detection tools have been developed; however, most of them only assess subpopulations expressing matching biomarkers. Thus, a combined detection system might be useful, and priority should be given to creating a universal panel that covers a broad spectrum of PCa subtypes. Expression of CD82 and several other markers carries prognostic value and correlates with stage and relapse risk. In metastatic PCa, CTC detection correlates with both survival and response to therapy. AR-V7 expression by CTCs is associated with resistance to androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (ARSis), while the response to taxanes remains unaffected. OCT4 expression in CTCs is associated with higher serum LDH levels, worse radiographic progression-free survival, and overall survival. Ezrin overexpression in CTCs is associated with bone metastasis in PCa patients. HER2-positive CTC detection correlates with worse overall and progression-free survival among patients treated with ARSis. Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) expression in CTCs was associated with metastases, lower overall survival and progression-free survival, as well as poor response to chemotherapy in mCRPCa. Overall, CTC detection for PCa screening, early diagnosis, and prognosis in localized disease offers limited added value. Conversely, in metastatic PCa it shows promising results as a prognostic marker. AR-V7 expression by CTCs is a predictor of response to ARS inhibitors, while PSMA expression in CTCs is associated with metastases, poor prognosis, and suboptimal treatment response. OCT4, HER2, and ezrin in CTCs were also predictors of poor prognosis and treatment response, but the data is limited. If explored further, microRNAs could serve as a potential alternative to circulating tumor cells, as they are - in theory - able to perform the same functions.
full_text:
A narrative review of the current state of circulating tumor cells in prostate cancer diagnostic

Circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in prostate cancer (PCa) have been an area of interest over the past 35 years. The aim of this narrative review is to summarize data on CTC application in PCa diagnosis. Several CTC detection tools have been developed; however, most of them only assess subpopulations expressing matching biomarkers. Thus, a combined detection system might be useful, and priority should be given to creating a universal panel that covers a broad spectrum of PCa subtypes. Expression of CD82 and several other markers carries prognostic value and correlates with stage and relapse risk. In metastatic PCa, CTC detection correlates with both survival and response to therapy. AR-V7 expression by CTCs is associated with resistance to androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (ARSis), while the response to taxanes remains unaffected. OCT4 expression in CTCs is associated with higher serum LDH levels, worse radiographic progression-free survival, and overall survival. Ezrin overexpression in CTCs is associated with bone metastasis in PCa patients. HER2-positive CTC detection correlates with worse overall and progression-free survival among patients treated with ARSis. Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) expression in CTCs was associated with metastases, lower overall survival and progression-free survival, as well as poor response to chemotherapy in mCRPCa. Overall, CTC detection for PCa screening, early diagnosis, and prognosis in localized disease offers limited added value. Conversely, in metastatic PCa it shows promising results as a prognostic marker. AR-V7 expression by CTCs is a predictor of response to ARS inhibitors, while PSMA expression in CTCs is associated with metastases, poor prognosis, and suboptimal treatment response. OCT4, HER2, and ezrin in CTCs were also predictors of poor prognosis and treatment response, but the data is limited. If explored further, microRNAs could serve as a potential alternative to circulating tumor cells, as they are – in theory – able to perform the same functions.

Today, diagnostic techniques such as MRI, CT, and ultrasonography are used for prostate cancer (PCa) diagnosis. However, they have several shortcomings. Ultrasonography lacks sensitivity and specificity, MRI is expensive, and CT is only suitable for assessing lymph nodes and is associated with radiation exposure, which renders these methods suboptimal for PCa screening. Biomarkers have been used as an evidence-based personalized tool for screening and prediction of survival, relapse after radical treatment, or response to particular therapy. Prostate-specific antigen (PSA), free PSA, prostate health index, and several other biomarkers are used in PCa diagnosis. Despite promising predictive values exhibited by several biomarkers, they have not been introduced into routine clinical practice. The reason for this is that their diagnostic value appears to be limited as they fail to provide any additional diagnostic information. Also, there is a lack of long-term data on cancer-specific outcomes.

A technique known as a “liquid biopsy” has become widespread in cancer management, diagnosis, risk stratification, and care personalization; it implies detection of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) and cell-independent factors in blood and other biological fluids. CTCs are cells that have separated from the primary tumor or metastatic lesion and entered the bloodstream. For more than 150 years after the first description, CTCs have been investigated in breast cancer, colorectal cancer, lung cancer, and many more. CTCs in PCa have been an area of research interest since 1988. Great progress has been made in the field of CTC identification in different disease stages and under various treatment modalities over the past four decades. The utility of CTCs was shown in a meta-analysis by Ma et al., who demonstrated a strong correlation between CTC count and overall survival (OS) or progression-free survival (PFS). More recent studies have focused on the expression of different receptors on CTC surface or gene expression in CTCs. In a systematic review, Casanova-Salas et al. focused on different blood-based biomarkers and revealed that CTCs play a substantial prognostic role in metastatic PCa.

Despite the emerging evidence surrounding CTCs in PCa, they have not yet found widespread application in clinical practice. Furthermore, no optimal technique of CTC detection has been developed due to a wide variety of detection methods with heterogeneous detection rates. In this review, we aim to shed light on the current state of CTCs, CTC-related biomarkers, and possible alternatives to CTCs in PCa. For this purpose, we conducted a literature search at MEDLINE (PubMed) and Google Scholar for articles in English without timeframe restriction.

CTC detection methods

Currently, there is no universal technique for detecting circulating tumor cells. Various approaches exist with different working principles and sensitivities (Figure 1). They are sometimes used in combination with each other.

CTC detection consists of two essential stages: enrichment (isolation) and detection proper (identification). Currently, there are 11 commercially available solutions with different working principles. Most of them are based on the capture of cells that express signs of epithelial differentiation. These can be EpCAM epithelial membrane markers (CellCollector) or cytokeratin expression (CellSearch). Several factors, such as low blood sample volume, mesenchymal transition of CTCs, and cloaking by stromal cells, platelets, and monocytes, might make CTC detection challenging. Various solutions exist to overcome these limitations, including blood nucleated cell analysis, reverse mRNA transcription in lysed blood cells, and direct bloodstream capture, among others. This means that due to the heterogeneity of CTC populations, different assays may detect different cellular subpopulations.

Physical isolation is used by EPIC Sciences, HD-CTC assay, ScreenCell, and ISET. The key idea behind this technique is to centrifuge cells and isolate CTCs with a specific size and weight. The main disadvantage is risk of missing CTC lines that are morphologically similar to normal cells.

During biological enrichment, antibodies are used to isolate CTCs (positive isolation) or remove other cells (negative isolation). The most commonly used and the only FDA-approved isolation method – CellSearch – is based on this technology. During the process of isolation, antibody-coated magnetic beads interact with cells expressing the epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM). After magnetic separation, cells may be stained with different fluorescent agents. Another method using antibody-coated magnetic beads is AdnaTest. Here, the cells are lysed, after which magnetic beads interact with mRNA, which is in turn transcribed into cDNA. Then, multiplex polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is used to detect gene expression.

CTCs may also be separated by microfluidics using different CTC-chips. The chip consists of an array of microposts, chemically functional with anti-epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM). Unfractionated blood flows through the chip under pressure, and CTCs expressing EpCAM on the surface of the cell are captured selectively. Recent studies have described microarray systems that do not require the use of adhesive molecules. Instead, CTC isolation occurs through hydrodynamic forces in the microchannels. This design classifies the chips as physical methods of isolation. The main advantage of this technique is its low cost. Furthermore, the microchip solution allows viable cells to be captured compared with magnetic-bead-based approaches that only obtain fixed non-viable cells.

CellSearch is the most common commercial CTC detection tool. The diagnostic accuracy of different systems is inconsistent, however. A study comparing AdnaTest, direct PCR detection, and CellSearch in mCRPCa patients revealed that AdnaTest and PCR had no significant disagreement (P=0.42), while AdnaTest+CellSearch and PCR+CellSearch had significant discrepancies (P=0.027 and P=0.001 respectively). CellSearch, CellCollector, and EPISPOT in high-risk localized PCa showed CTC detection rates of 37%, 54.9%, and 58.7% respectively. Total agreement was only reached in 37.4% of cases, and paired agreement was reached in 56–60%. CTC count detected by EPISPOT correlated with PSA level and disease stage, whereas CellSearch and CellCollector showed no correlation with these factors. In another study comparing CellSearch with fluoro-EPISPOTPSA/FGF2 and CellCollector, CTC detection rates were 23.9%, 52%, and 57.7% respectively. Interestingly, the number of CTCs detected with CellSearch showed a positive correlation with Gleason score of ≥8. For fluoro-EPISPOT PSA/FGF2 and CellCollector, this association was not observed. Given this heterogeneous data, it can be assumed that each CTC detection tool only assesses subpopulations expressing matching biomarkers. Thus, using combined detection systems might be useful, and priority should be given to creating standard panels for each cancer type.

CTC biomarkers detection methods

Apart from detection of CTCs themselves, expression of several biomarkers, e.g., proteins, receptors (for example, AR-V7 or PSMA), in tumor cells may be assessed to obtain additional valuable data. They show promise owing to their association with overall and progression-free survival.

Similar to CTC detection, biomarker assessment may be based on different principles. Among them, the most common are PCR and immunofluorescence. The former is usually considered to be more sensitive but also more expensive and time-consuming.

PSMA detection methods

In a study by Gorges et al., the CellSearch®-PSMA assay was used with BioLegend antibody (clone LNI-17) for PSMA detection in CTCs. Nagaya et al. and Francolini et al. used the reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR). AdnaTest PrimerMix ProstateDetect was used for PSMA amplification. Yang et al. incubated CTCs with APC-labeled anti- PSMA antibody (Biolegend, Cat: 342508) and detected them with ImageStreamX® Mark II imaging flow cytometry system (Amnis, Seattle, WA, USA).

AR-V7 detection methods

Sepe et al. used the AR-V7 RTPCR kit (AR-V7 assay RT-PCR, Bird, Monteriggioni, Italy) for AR-V7 expression evaluation. Antonarakis et al. also assessed AR-V7 expression by means of RT-PCR but used custom primers. Scher et al. employed immunofluorescence staining for AR-V7 (EPR15656; Abcam). Mailliet et al. used the RT-PCR kit for AR-V7 detection on CTCs.

OCT4 detection methods

Octamer-binding transcription factor 4 (OCT4)-positive CTCs were identified using RNA in situ hybridization of OCT4 and epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM). Multiplex RNA in situ hybridization methods were used in a study by Yang et al. to assess the expression of CD133 stemness marker in CTCs in PCa.

Ezrin detection methods

Overexpression of ezrin (cytovillin, villin2), a component of the cell membrane and membrane organizer, in CTCs was identified using immunohistochemistry analysis (ezrin 1:200 dilution; Abcam, USA) and qRT-PCR with ezrin-forward and ezrin-reverse primers (5ʹ-AGCTGTGAAGAGACTCTGTTTG-3ʹ; CTTAGCTGTGAAGGAGAAAGC-3ʹ).

HER2 detection methods

Maillet et al. used RT-PCR and the QuantiNova™ SYBR® Green RT-PCR kit for detection of HER2, a member of the tyrosine kinase receptor family involved in progression of CRPCa.

Diagnostic value of CTCS for PCa screening and prognosis

СТСs in PCa screening

There are several papers assessing the diagnostic value of CTCs as a screening method for PCa. Overall negative predictive value (NPV) is 57.4–98%, and positive predictive value (PPV) is 18–65%. Low PPV limits their use for screening purposes. Although CTCs have been detected in both localized (including low- and intermediate-risk) and metastatic PCa, the utility of CTCs in the localized PCa is more limited.

СTC count in PCa and intraprostatic intervention

Interestingly, minimally invasive interventions, such as brachytherapy, lead to a temporary increase in CTC count. In a trial by Tsumura et al., no CTCs were found before the procedure; however, immediately after brachytherapy, 11.8% of patients were CTC-positive. This may be due to stromal disruption causing epithelial cells to enter the bloodstream. The same effect is observed after transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy. The practical application of this finding remains disputable.

СTC count in PCa and clinical features

In general, CTC count did not correlate with clinical features, such as PSA level, stage, nodal status, and Gleason score in localized disease. After adding in other CTC-related markers, however, CTCs carried greater prognostic value. In particular, the CD82 tumor suppressor gene is involved in cell adhesion to the protein matrix. In a study by Murray et al., 36-, 60-, and 120-month biochemical-free survival for CTC+ CD82− was 93%, 93%, and 69%, respectively, while for CTC+ CD82+ these values were 62%, 44%, and 16%, respectively. Therefore, CD82 expression points to a similar prognosis as the absence of CTCs. Another CTC-related marker is AR expression. In a study by Salami et al., significantly more AR+ CTCs were found in patients with metastatic progression and biochemical relapse.

Most researchers report no correlation between baseline CTC counts and stage in patients undergoing radical prostatectomy. Nevertheless, Liu et al. noticed that a number of bi-phenotypical CTCs (cells that have markers of both epithelial and mesenchymal cells) predicted extraprostatic extension, P<0.01, OR=1.383, AUC 0.72.

СTCs in PCa and treatment response after radical prostatectomy

СТСs can also be used to monitor response to treatment. Murray et al. studied blood and bone marrow of 321 patients with localized PCa one month after radical prostatectomy for CTCs and micrometastases. CTC+ patients with micrometastases in the bone marrow had increased early failure compared to those with micrometastases only. Therefore, these two forms of minimal residual disease could be used to guide clinical decision making in patients at a high risk of PCa recurrence. After radical prostatectomy in patients with high-risk PCa, CTC count ≥1 was a significant predictor of worse 5-year OS but not metastasis-free survival.

Thus, CTC are detectable even at early stages of PCa, but the evidence is limited. Expression of CD82 among other markers carries prognostic value and correlates with stage. CTC detection may be also applied in patients at high risk of relapse. However, practical applications for these findings remain a distant prospect.

СTC count in mCRPCa and disease prognosis

Metastatic PCa might be the most appropriate subtype for CTC detection. Cell counts have been found to correlate with overall survival. Among patients with cell counts of ≥5 cells/7.5mL, Okegawa et al. reported a median OS of 11.5 vs. 20 mo., Vogelzang et al. noted that CTC counts of ≥5 cells/7.5mL significantly correlated with lower OS in patients with mCRPCa (HR 3.23, P=0.0028). Thakur et al. found OS of 14.6 months in mCRPCa patients with ≥5 circulating cells/7.5ml vs. 26.6 months in patients with <5 circulating cells/7.5 mL, Lorente et al. found that OS was 22 months for <5 CTCs/7.5mL and 11.1 months for ≥5 CTCs/7.5mL (P<0.001), Belderbos et al. reported 6.9 months OS for ≥5 CTCs/7.5 mL (IQR: 4.3–13.8) vs. 22.3 months OS for <5 CTCs/7.5 mL (IQR: 19.2–34.6), and Wang et al. noted median OS of 6.0 months for <5 CTCs/7.5 mL vs. not reached in ≥5 CTCs/7.5 mL (log-rank P<0.0001), with 6.90-fold (HR 6.90, 95% CI 2.72–17.47) greater risk of death in patients with poor prognosis.

In the CARD trial (NCT02485691) high (>3) CTC counts at the screening among patients with mCRPCa were associated with shorter OS (P=0.03). Also, higher CTC counts at the baseline were accompanied by elevated LDH (P=0.014), PSA (P<0.001), and alkaline phosphatase (P<0.001).

A panel of biomarkers containing CTC count and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) level was used to determine overall 2-year survival in the COU-AA-301 trial. Then followed a multinational, randomized, double-blind phase III trial of abiraterone acetate plus prednisone versus prednisone alone in patients with metastatic CRPCa previously treated with docetaxel. Patients with CTC counts of <5/7.5 mL and patients with CTC counts of >5/7.5 mL + LDH>250 U/L showed dramatic difference in survival (46% vs. 2%, respectively).

Interestingly, there is evidence that long-term use of simvastatin can inhibit the metastatic potential of PCa cells. Research conducted by Kafka et al. revealed that patients diagnosed with metastatic PCa receiving simvastatin over an extended period exhibited lower levels of CTCs. Moreover, in-vitro experiments on the PC-3 PCa cell line showed that simvastatin inhibited cell growth and migration capabilities.

СTC count in mCRPCa and treatment response

CTC counts were also inversely proportional to treatment response. For example, patients resistant to abiraterone or enzalutamide had significantly higher CTC counts compared to responders. Goldkorn et al. carried out a phase III trial on docetaxel with or without atrasentan for metastatic castration-resistant PCa. They reported that baseline CTC count added to the standard panel of examinations increased survival predictive accuracy by 8–10%. Day-0 CTC count of >5 per 7.5 mL was associated with poorer treatment response, higher baseline PSA and alkaline phosphatase levels, and bone pain and liver disease. Antonarakis et al. found the response rate among CTC-negative patients to be 75.5%, compared to only 52.2% among CTC-positive patients. Radiological response was also lower in the CTC-positive group compared to the CTC-negative group (66% vs. 25%). In CTC+ patients with CRPCa, persistence of СTCs in the peripheral blood after one cycle of cabazitaxel was strongly associated with poorer OS, as reported by Koinis et al. The analysis of CTC counts in the CARD trial (NCT02485691), which focuses on cabazitaxel treatment for patients with metastatic castration-resistant PCA and progression after receiving docetaxel and alternative androgen-signaling-targeted inhibitors (within 12 months), has revealed that tumor response was linked to a decrease in CTC count from high (≥3) at screening to low (<3) at the second cycle of cabazitaxel treatment (P=0.007).

To date, there is only one paper on CTC status after cytoreductive prostatectomy for patients with oligometastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. This work shows a significant decrease in median CTC counts after prostatectomy (4 vs. 6, P=0.026). For patients with CTC increase after surgery, the PSA response rate was lower (73.0% vs. 99.8%, P=0.162). PSA nadir was higher (0.043 vs. 0.003 ng/mL, P=0.072), but there were no differences in clinical, pathological or oncologic outcomes at 71.6 months compared to patients with CTC decrease. Cryosurgery for metastatic hormone-sensitive PCa combined with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) also resulted in decreased CTC count at 3 months (z=2.170, P=0.030) and 12 months (z=2.481; P=0.013) compared with CTC counts in the group that only received ADT. These patients also had better PFS (HR 0.062, 95% CI, 0.048–0.080). This data indicates a potential benefit of prostate surgery for CTC+ hormone-sensitive mPCa patients. However, further high-quality studies are required (Table I).

CTC biomarkers and prognosis, treatment response

CTCs also express different markers (receptors, genes) whose detection could help to predict survival and response to treatment. Several authors highlighted substantial influence of AR-V7 on OS and PFS, while others found no such association. AR-V7 expression did not influence OS according to Belderbos et al. (HR 1.1, 95% CI 0.6–1.9, P=0.78) and Sharp et al. (HR 1.26, 95% CI 0.73–2.17; P=0.4), as well as response to radiotherapy. It should be noted that AR-V7-positive patients showed reduced response to ARSis, while the response to taxane therapy (docetaxel, cabazitaxel, paclitaxel) remained unaffected. Scher et al. reported median progression-free survival of 2.3 months in the ARS group vs. 14.5 in the taxane group (P<0.001) and median OS of 4.6 months vs. not reached (P<0.001). Response to enzalutamide or abiraterone was significantly lower in the AR-V7-positive group (13.9% vs. 52.2%). With respect to ARSi resistance, Tommasi et al. reported that AR-V7+ patients had resistance in 75% cases compared to only 57% in AR-V7-negative patients. In a study by Crespo et al., inferior outcomes after abiraterone and enzalutamide treatment were associated with increased CTC median nuclear AR intensity (17.8 in abiraterone- and enzalutamide-naïve patients, 18.7 in patients with progression who received abiraterone and enzalutamide treatment, and 23.3 in patients who received abiraterone or enzalutamide treatment but discontinued it due to progression).

Octamer-binding transcription factor 4 (OCT4)-positive CTC-positive patients with CRPCa treated with abiraterone + prednisone showed visceral metastases and high LDH serum levels, worse radiographic progression-free and OS compared with OCT4-negative CTC-positive and CTC-negative patients.

Overexpression of ezrin (cytovillin, villin2), a component of the cell membrane and membrane organizer, in CTCs was associated with PCa metastases. In a study by Chen et al., ezrin was upregulated in PCa tissue samples, and mRNA expression was the highest in the bone metastasis group. 22RV1 and PC-3 cell lines transfected with ezrin-overexpressed plasmid had higher migration and invasion abilities than the control 22RV1 and PC-3 cell lines. Among the patients with bone metastasis included in the study, ezrin-positive CTCs were detected more frequently than in PCa with lymph node metastasis or localized PCa (12% vs. 3.5% vs. 0.2%, P<0.01).

In a study by Yang et al., CD133 stemness marker detection in CTCs in patients with newly-diagnosed castratesensitive metastatic PCa has been identified as an unfavorable prognostic factor. Of the CTC+ CD133+ patients, 93.3% progressed to castration-resistant PCa, while in the CTC-positive CD133− and CTC-negative groups progression was only 73.3% and 75.0%, respectively (P=0.043). Also, this group had the lowest progression-free survival compared with the others (10 vs. 13 vs. 14 months respectively, P=0.022).

Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) expression in CTCs has attracted much attention recently. Gorges et al. were the first to describe PSMA detection in CTCs with the FDA-approved CellSearch system. PSMA expression was assessed in PCa cell lines, and CTCs were taken from blood of two patient cohorts. PSMA expression was higher in patients with higher Gleason scores (P<0.01) and metastases in lymph nodes (P<0.01) or bones (P<0.01). PSMA expression in CTCs was associated with metastatic PCa and early biochemical relapse as opposed to localized disease before treatment. Among the patients with CRPCa, PSMA expression in CTCs was associated with age (P=0.045), baseline PSA (P=0.023) and prior use of enzalutamide (P<0.01). Treatment response revealed an inverse correlation between PSMA-positive cases and decrease in PSA (median change: −90.9% vs. −13.8%, P<0.01). PSA-PFS (12 weeks for PSMA-positive vs. 30 weeks for PSMA-negative, P=0.008) and OS (13 months for PSMA-positive vs. 27 months for PSMA-negative, P=0.01) were significantly shorter in the PSMA+ group. PSMA in CTCs was an independent predictor of the best PSA response after treatment switching (P<0.001) and shorter OS (hazard ratio with PSMA+=7.62; 95% CI, 1.08–15.3; P=0.04). A combination of PSMA and Gleason Score was a predictor of shorter PSA-PFS (hazard ratio with PSMA+=4.02; 95% CI, 1.33–12.8; P=0.014). Among patients with mCRPCa undergoing standard treatment (ARTAs [androgen receptor targeted agents] + ADT), PSMA expression had no impact on median time to castration resistance and biochemical response to therapy. PSMA+ patients had higher risk of progression on abiraterone/enzalutamide and the lowest OS (26, 21, and 11 months for males without CTCs, PSMA− CTCs, and PSMA+ CTCs). HR for OS and PFS were 3.0 (95% CI=1.1–7.8) and 2.3 (95% CI=0.9–5.8), respectively. For PSMA-targeted radioligand therapy for advanced mCRPCa, PSMA+ CTC detection was complementary to whole-body PET-PSMA results in disease phenotyping. Detection of a high fraction of PSMA-negative CTCs was used as a predictor of a poor radioligand therapy response (OR: 0.9379 [95% CI, 0.8558–0.9902]; P=0.0160), shorter PFS (OR: 1.236 [95% CI, 1.035–2.587]; P=0.0043), and shorter OS (OR: 1.056 [95% CI, 1.008–1.141]; P=0.0182).

Detection of HER2 (a member of the tyrosine kinase receptor family which is involved in CRPCa progression) in CTCs of mCRPCa patients was linked to poorer oncological outcomes, including PSA-progression-free survival (6.2 months compared to 13.0 months, P=0.034), radiological progression-free survival (6.8 months compared to 25.6 months, P=0.022), and OS (22.7 months compared to median not reached, P=0.05) when treated with ARSis.

Ongoing clinical trials on CTCs

A search on clinicaltrials.gov yielded 53 recruiting CTC studies dealing with various aspects of PCa diagnosis and treatment. In the majority of them, CTC count and CTC-related biomarkers are assessed among other conventional methods of PCa diagnosis. However, there is also a study on a “new technique” of CTC isolation (NCT03979339, no details about the technique are provided), and several trials specifically focusing on the predictive value of CTCs for metastatic PCa prognosis, risk of metastases and even early diagnosis of PCa.

Micro-RNAs as a possible alternative to CTC identification in PCa

Other potential circulating biomarkers for PCa diagnosis and prognosis are microRNAs (miRNAs), short noncoding single-stranded molecules of RNA capable of regulating gene expression. miRNAs inhibit target mRNA translation by binding with the 3’, 5’ untranslated region or coding sequence and by direct or indirect activation of gene expression. The first report on the association between circulating serum miRNAs and PCa was published in 2011 by Moltzahn et al. Identification of 384 miRNAs was performed in 12 healthy individuals and 36 patients from low-, intermediate- and high-risk PCa groups according to the Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment (CAPRA) score. miR-106a and miR-1274 showed promising diagnostic potential. Among circulating miRNAs, miR-141 and miR-375 upregulation is the most commonly found association with PCa. Their expression level correlated with Gleason score, tumor stage, and even castration resistance. On the other hand, Canatan et al. reported a significant decrease in miR141 levels in PCa patients compared to healthy individuals (P=0.004). Conversely, there was a marked increase in miR155 levels in PCa (P=0.005). Promising results were obtained by Heydari et al. regarding the association between circulating miR222–3p and PCa. In the study, detection of miR222–3p in plasma had a sensitivity of 75%, a specificity of 74%, and an AUC of 0.73 in PCa detection. A prediction model containing three circulating miRNAs (miR-3147, miR-4513, and miR-4728–5p), stage and Gleason score was able to predict recurrence after radical prostatectomy. To develop the model, Takamori et al. used Fisher’s linear discriminant analysis. The AUC of the model was 0.8, sensitivity was 0.78, and specificity was 0.76. Kachris et al. conducted a study to evaluate the serum levels of miR-21, miR-106b, miR-141, and miR-375 in patients with PCa before and after radiotherapy (primary, adjuvant or salvage). They found that miR-141 and miR-375 expression did not show any association with clinicopathological features or treatment outcome. However, low levels of circulating miR-106b were associated with tumor invasion into the seminal vesicles and extracapsular extension (P=0.044 and 0.031, respectively). Higher serum levels of miR-21 and miR-106b were associated with biochemical relapse in the salvage group after radiotherapy (P=0.043, P=0.032, respectively). Overexpression of miR-21 before radiotherapy and miR-106b after radiotherapy was associated with worse OS (P=0.049, 0.05 respectively). Work is underway to develop a universal miRNA panel to detect cancers of the prostate and other genitourinary organs and assess prognosis. Sequeira et al. aimed to identify and distinguish between genitourinary cancers, specifically prostate, kidney, and bladder cancers. To achieve this, they measured the levels of various microRNAs (hsa-miR-126–3p, hsa-miR-141–3p, hsa-miR-153–5p, hsa-miR-155–5p, hsa-miR-182–5p, hsa-miR-205–5p, and hsa-miR-375–3p) in plasma samples obtained from healthy individuals and patients with renal cell carcinoma, bladder cancer, and PCa. The microRNA panel (hsa-miR-182–5p + has-miR-375–3p) exhibited a sensitivity of 64.93% and a specificity of 81.08% in detection of urological cancers, with a PPV of 92.55%. To distinguish between PCa patients and others, circulating levels of miR-126–3p were evaluated, resulting in an 81.5% NPV and accuracy of 52.6%. Similarly, the same circulating miRNA showed a specificity of 83.4% and accuracy of 71.57% when differentiating between PCa and bladder cancer or renal cell carcinoma in the advanced stage group. Given the low likelihood of the disease, a circulating miRNA panel (miR-302a-5 and/or miR-367–3p) paired with clinical data and MRI offers a prostate biopsy avoidance rate of approximately 20%. Circulating miRNA serum levels in patients with mCRPCa could be used to gauge response to docetaxel. The AUC for a combination of miR-183–5p and miR-21–5p in assessment of response to docetaxel therapy was 0.82, while PSA only offered 0.79. A combination of these two miRNAs and PSA yielded AUC of 1.00 in differentiating between response and non-response to docetaxel.

Limitations of the study

Withing present investigation we couldn’t conduct a meta-analysis due to wide variety of applied methods and outcomes. Among the manuscripts on similar topic, a comprehensive review published on 2021 should be mentioned. It describes the clinical role of CTC in PCa. Our review updates current data on this topic. Besides, we paid a special attention to CTC detection methods and highlighted the potential applications of detecting circulating microRNA as an alternative for diagnosis, predicting the course and response to therapy of PCa.

Conclusions

Overall, CTC detection for PCa screening, early detection, and prognosis in localized disease offers limited added value. Conversely, in metastatic PCa it shows promising results as a prognostic marker. AR-V7 expression by CTCs is a predictor of response to ARS inhibitors, while PSMA expression in CTCs is associated with metastases, poor prognosis, and suboptimal treatment response. OCT4, HER2, and ezrin in CTCs are also predictors of poor prognosis and treatment response, but the data is limited. If explored further, microRNAs could serve as a potential PCa biomarker, but it is still unclear whether they can substitute CTCs or provide additional data.

Conflicts of interest

The authors certify that there is no conflict of interest with any financial organization regarding the material discussed in the manuscript.
